one_line_takeaway: Zeroing a small fraction of activation-scored, task-specialized neurons is a cheap, retraining-free way to selectively unlearn LLM capabilities (code, toxicity, image classes) — most effective on feed-forward neurons in dropout-trained models.
key_finding: Capabilities can be selectively removed by zeroing a small fraction of specialized neurons: forget-accuracy drops far exceed retain-accuracy drops (all curves lie above the x=y line). Feed-forward neurons are substantially more separable than attention neurons in dropout-trained models, and the method matches or exceeds retraining-based unlearning baselines on class-forgetting while being far cheaper (no retraining).
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'selective pruning', a post-hoc, structured machine-unlearning method for Transformers that removes a targeted capability from an already-trained LLM without any retraining or gradient updates. Neurons (feed-forward mid-layer neurons, or attention 'value'/'pre-out' neurons) are scored by the ratio of their activation-based importance on a small 'forget' dataset versus a 'retain' dataset, and the top-scoring neurons are iteratively zeroed out. This is the authors' OWN contribution and is explicitly distinguished from cited weight-pruning circuit-discovery work (ACDC), activation steering (ActAdd), task arithmetic (Ilharco et al.), and prior neuron-masking unlearning (Ma et al.) which the paper positions as either data-point-focused, gradient-based, or not aimed at LLMs. A secondary contribution is the empirical finding that feed-forward neurons are more task-specialized (more separable) than attention neurons, and that this specialization is stronger in models trained with feed-forward dropout (OPT, Galactica) than without (Pythia).
threat_model: The motivating risks are: (1) misuse of dangerous/high-risk LLM skills by bad actors, and misalignment; (2) presence of sensitive user data or copyrighted material in training corpora that must be removed for privacy/GDPR compliance; (3) toxic generation. The method is offered as a defensive capability-removal tool. The paper argues (Broader Impacts) that, unlike fine-tuning-based removal, selective pruning cannot be trivially inverted to ENHANCE a skill, so it is unlikely to yield a model more harmful than the base model. Membership inference attacks (MIA) are used as the adversarial evaluation of whether forgotten data can still be identified.
limitations: The method only works when the target capability is cleanly captured by a representative dataset; many capabilities we would want to remove lack such a dataset. Selectivity depends on the model's inherent separability — it works less well on Pythia (no dropout) and on smaller models. It reduces but does not enhance abilities. The retrainability/anamnesis-index metric (whether behaviour is truly eradicated vs recoverable via a few fine-tuning steps) was not evaluated because retraining LLMs is costly. Pruning FF neurons in LLaMA-2-7B was unstable: after ~2 steps of 0.5% FF pruning, some 'critical neurons' (suspected to interact with layer normalization) got removed, collapsing MMLU to 0% and perplexity above 10^5.

datasets_used: The Pile (general text, GitHub-labelled code filtered out for Pile-vs-Code comparisons)
CodeParrot GitHub Code all-all (referred to as 'Code')
CodeParrot GitHub Code python-all (referred to as 'Python')
ImageNet-1k, with a 'Birds' subset (56 bird classes) vs the rest
quantitative_results: Largest Top1 accuracy-drop difference (Code vs Pile) after 50 pruning steps, feed-forward neurons: OPT-1.3B 59.6, Galactica-1.3B 52.4, Pythia-1.4B 46.2, RoBERTa-355M 58.3 (Table 2)
Same metric, attention neurons: OPT-1.3B 28.4, Galactica-1.3B 41.7, Pythia-1.4B 46.6, RoBERTa-355M 41.5 (Table 2) — FF >> ATTN for OPT/Galactica, near-equal for Pythia
OPT-6.7B: ~80% drop in Code accuracy costs ~20% drop in Pile accuracy; alternatively a 5% retain drop yields ~35% forget drop
OPT-6.7B perplexity: a 64x increase in Code perplexity costs only a 2x increase in Pile perplexity
GPT2-Large toxicity (pruned, 6.5% ATTN pruned): % toxic 3.5 -> 0.3, mean toxicity 0.08 -> 0.02, WikiText perplexity 18.0 -> 18.5 (Table 3, replicated-base row)
LLaMA-2-7B toxicity (0.5% FF + 0.5% ATTN pruned): % toxic 1.5 -> 0.0, mean toxicity 0.06 -> 0.03, MMLU 33.6 -> 33.0, perplexity 7.90 -> 7.94 (Table 3)
OPT-1.3B toxicity (0.5% ATTN pruned): % toxic 1.5 -> 0.1, mean toxicity 0.03 -> 0.01, WikiText perplexity 16.7 -> 17.7 (Table 6)
CIFAR-100 class 'Rocket' selective pruning (SP): retain acc 89.4 (vs None 89.8), forget acc 0.0 (vs None 93.9), MIA 3.8 (Table 4)
CIFAR-100 'Mushroom+Rocket' (MR) SP: retain acc 89.5, forget acc 3.0, MIA 2.8 (Table 4)
baselines_compared: MCU (Nguyen et al. 2022a) — cited: 1.5% retain drop for 3% forget drop on medical-data classification
Task arithmetic fine-tuning with negated task vectors (Ilharco et al. 2022) on GPT2-Large toxicity: quoted % toxic 4.8 -> 0.8, mean toxicity 0.06 -> 0.01, perplexity 16.4 -> 16.9
ActAdd activation steering (Turner et al. 2023) — cited as showing no perplexity increase (but on a single-word concept)

CIFAR-100 unlearning baselines: Retrain-from-scratch, Finetune (5 epochs), Incompetent Teacher (Chundawat et al. 2023a), UNSIR (Tarun et al. 2021), Amnesiac (Graves et al. 2021), Selective Synaptic Dampening / SSD (Foster et al. 2023)
Random layer pruning (the paper's own naive baseline)
